the mapping, I think, is the part that a lot of people kind of like, I don't know if they take it for granted, but it's really around like there's the company context, right?

So for a CIO, the primary pain point is, I mean, we sort of joke about, you know, agents gone wild, right?
Every leader is being hammered on how they're going to use AI.
Everybody's bought their own, you know, HR, you know, there's an agent for pre-screening candidates.
There's an agent for scheduling interviews.
There's an agent for procurement requests, right?
So, like, there's agents everywhere in a business.
CIOs have little to no control over them.
And so, showing a CIO how we are the platform through which they own the control of the agents.
And this is where it gets a little bit esoteric, but, I mean, but it is an important differentiator.
And that is that...
You know, fundamentally, agents built on LLMs are probabilistic in nature.
That's how that technology works.
Ask ChatGPT the same question twice, you get two answers.
That's fantastic for a lot of uses of agentic technology.
When you're talking about processing appeals for healthcare claims, you cannot have a probabilistic owner of that process, right?
It has to be predictable, consistent, repeatable, auditable, right?
An agent, if you spool an agent up and you just literally say, should I accept or reject this appeal?
It's completely non-starter in any regulated industry, right?
You have no idea how they arrive at that decision point.
So it's kind of this combination of control generally, and then the fact that in enterprise processes where you need repeat...
Readability and scale and consistency.
You can't just have an agent do the work.
It needs to operate in the context of an actual deterministic workflow engine.
And like Google doesn't have it.
Microsoft doesn't have it.
Snowflake doesn't have it, right?
I mean, all these companies that are pitching agents to sort of own work, you can't have an agent run work at scale where you need a consistent outcome.
So for the CIO, it's a ton of that.
It's give me a way to control and know that I'm going to have predictable outcomes.

For a chief data officer, it's a little different angle in that, you know, for our ICP, they have all invested tens of millions of dollars getting their data into Snowflake or Databricks or AWS or BigQuery.
And the way that they're getting value is dashboards, basically.
So for...
Chief Data Officer, what we're saying is, look, you've already done all the hard part.
If you can get insights from this data, great.

Well, now let's actually take action against that data natively and drive tremendous measurable value, right, where you're able to end your contract for the BPO or you're able to process claims on it, right?
So it's actually this big, like, 10x the ROI on the money that you've already spent because you're actually taking action against it instead of just using it to gain insights.
